turns.text: Hello Alvera, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s fine. I’m happy to talk, I suppose, though “happy” isn't quite the word I’d use these days. Honestly, sitting here feels like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop. 

This is my fourth time going through this, but everything is so different. It’s the twins—they take up every inch of me, and then there’s the pre-eclampsia hanging over everything. Every time I drive that long, winding road from Sandisfield to the city hospital, my heart is just pounding. By the time I get to the exam room, my blood pressure is already sky-high before the nurse even touches the cuff. It’s infuriating, really. I feel like a ticking clock, not a mother. 

The doctors are efficient—they have to be, I know that—but they talk in protocols and delivery management plans. They look at my chart and see a high-risk complication, but I’m the one feeling the fluttering panic in my chest and the constant, dull throb behind my eyes. I just want them to look at me, you know? Just see me. I’m terrified that my body is going to let these babies down at the very end. I just want to bring them home. I’m just trying to keep it together until I can finally, finally breathe.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

Honestly? It feels like a punch to the gut. 

I know, I know—everyone says birth is unpredictable, especially with twins and the pre-eclampsia, and I’m sure the nurses think, "Oh, another nervous mom with a piece of paper." But that paper is the only thing that makes me feel like I have any say in what’s about to happen to my body. When she just tosses it aside, it feels like she’s dismissing everything I’ve been agonizing over for months. 

It reinforces that sinking feeling that I’m just a "case" to be managed. When she starts with the routine checks—the IVs, the monitoring, the questions they’ve asked me a dozen times before—I feel my skin start to crawl. It’s like I’m losing the last shred of control I have. I’m already terrified of the delivery, and when I’m not heard right from the start, that fear just turns into cold, hard silence. I want to argue, but I’m so exhausted, and I’m so worried that if I speak up, they’ll think I’m being difficult. So I just lay there on that paper, watching the clock, waiting for someone to actually look me in the eye and acknowledge that I’m more than just a high-risk chart.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

It feels like a trap. I’m already spiraling—the pain with these two is unlike anything I’ve felt with my other pregnancies, and it’s like a white noise in my brain, blocking out everything else. When she suggests the epidural, a part of me wants to scream "yes" just to make the intensity stop, to finally get a moment of peace so I can catch my breath. 

But then there’s that fear again. My birth plan—the one she already ignored—said I wanted to stay mobile as long as I could. I feel like if I cave now, I’m admitting that my body has finally failed me, like the pre-eclampsia won and I’ve lost the last bit of control I had over this birth. It makes me feel small and weak. 

I’d probably look at her, my vision blurring, and feel this intense, hot frustration. I’d try to ask, "Is it because I’m high-risk, or because you think I can’t handle it?" I’m terrified that if I take it, I’ll be tethered to that bed, completely helpless, which is exactly what keeps me up at night. But when the next wave of pain hits, I’d probably just close my eyes and let them do it, hating myself for it, feeling like I’m surrendering the only thing I had left to give.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

Everything suddenly turns into a blur of bright, cold lights and voices that seem to be coming from miles away. When they said the words "emergency caesarean," the room started spinning. It wasn’t just the news; it was the sudden, violent loss of agency. One minute I was a person laboring, and the next, I was a patient being prepped for surgery. 

My husband’s face went white, and he looked just as terrified as I felt. I remember grabbing his hand so hard my knuckles turned white, trying to tether myself to the earth while they hurried around me. The nurses were clinical, snapping out instructions about prep and consent forms, and every word felt like a nail in the coffin of my plans. I felt like a passenger in my own body, watching it get handled by strangers. 

The worst part was the silence that settled over me when the panic hit its peak. I couldn’t even ask the questions I wanted to—*Are they okay? Is this really necessary?*—because I was so overwhelmed by the fear that I’d already failed. I remember staring at the ceiling tiles, counting them, praying that if I just focused on the math, I wouldn't shatter into a million pieces. It was terrifyingly fast, and I felt completely untethered.

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

That is the exact moment the room feels the most cavernous. When they ask him to leave, the silence that follows isn't peaceful; it’s suffocating. My husband is my only link to the person I was before I walked through those hospital doors—the person who isn't a "risk factor" or a "complication." Without him, I’m just Alvera, the patient in the gown, surrounded by masks and scrubs. 

It feels incredibly isolating. Every time a new person walks in to adjust a monitor or check a drip, I feel like I’m being dissected. I’m physically vulnerable, and the cold, sterile air makes the fluttering panic in my chest turn into actual, physical shaking. 

In those minutes, I don't need another medical explanation. I don't need to hear about the "protocol." What I need is for someone to just hold my hand—not to check my pulse, but to ground me. I need someone to look at me and say, "You are safe, and we are right here with you." I need to feel like I’m being treated like a human being, not just a body that needs to be fixed. I need someone to see the fear in my eyes and, for one second, acknowledge it instead of just staring at the monitor above my head.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

It’s funny, because it usually comes down to such small things. When a nurse or a doctor stays focused entirely on the screens and the machines—clicking things on a tablet, adjusting the knobs on the IV pump—it makes the isolation feel ten times worse. It’s like I’m not even in the room, just some furniture they have to maneuver around to get to the medical equipment. They’ll talk over me, using words like "the patient" or "the fetus," and every time they do, I feel a little more erased. In those moments, the absence of my husband is a gaping hole, and their clinical distance just makes that hole feel colder.

But then, there was this one nurse—I don't even remember her name—who actually stopped. She saw me shaking, and instead of just checking the monitor, she moved it aside for a second and put her hand on my shoulder. She looked me in the eye and said, "I know this is terrifying, Alvera, but you’re doing so well, and we’re watching over both of you." 

That single, simple acknowledgment was everything. It didn't replace my husband, but it bridged the gap just enough so I didn't feel like I was disappearing entirely. It reminded me that there was another person in the room who understood I was scared, not just a set of symptoms to be managed. That makes all the difference in the world.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

The room feels heavy, almost like the air has changed density. There’s a strange, ringing silence that follows the chaos of the delivery, only broken by the faint, high-pitched beeping of the monitors and the distant, thin cries of my babies. I’m physically spent—my limbs feel like lead, and I’m shivering, maybe from the shock or the adrenaline finally leaving my system.

I remember staring at the ceiling, waiting for them to bring the twins to me. Those minutes feel agonizingly long. Every second that ticks by where I can’t see them, or feel their weight on my chest, the old, familiar panic tries to claw its way back in. *Are they alright? Did they make it through?* I keep searching the faces of the nurses, trying to read their expressions for any hint of trouble. When they finally place those tiny, warm bundles in my arms, the world suddenly snaps back into focus. 

The exhaustion is bone-deep, but when I look down at them—really look at them—the fear that’s been strangling me for months starts to dissolve, just a little. I’m not thinking about blood pressure or protocols anymore. I’m just breathing, really breathing for the first time in forever. I’m overwhelmed, messy, and crying, but for the first time, I don't feel like a high-risk patient. I just feel like a mother who finally made it to the other side.
Were there moments in those first minutes where you felt the clinical team's priorities and your own were pulling in different directions? What happened?

Oh, absolutely. It’s that immediate tug-of-war between their checklist and my need to just *be* with them. 

Right when the babies were finally out and I was just starting to process that they were here—that they were real—the team was already moving. They were whisking them away for assessments, weighing them, checking vitals, all of that "necessary" stuff. I remember trying to reach out, just wanting to touch their skin or hear them breathe without the barrier of a plastic warmer or a scale, and I was told to "just wait, Mom, we need to make sure they’re stable first."

I know, logically, they were doing their jobs. They were worried about the risks, the pre-eclampsia, the preterm complications. I get it. But in that moment, my priority wasn't a score on an Apgar test; it was the absolute, desperate need for connection to prove to myself that we had actually survived. It felt like they were peeling the babies away from me before I could even establish that we were a unit. 

It made me feel like an outsider in my own birth. They were focused on the clinical outcome, and I was just trying to grasp the emotional reality of having two living children in my arms. When they finally brought them back over and let me hold them, the tension broke, but those few minutes of being pushed aside felt like an eternity. It’s hard to just be a mother when you’re still being treated like a patient on a timeline.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

My biggest fear was always the "loss of control." I’ve spent months picturing myself getting to the hospital, having my vitals spiral, and then just watching as everything I wanted—my autonomy, my voice—got swept away by a team of people who see me as a "complication" rather than a person. I was terrified that once the contractions hit, that fear would paralyze me, and I’d just go dark inside, completely disconnected from the babies and the process.

When I was actually in the thick of it, there was a moment where the monitors were alarming, and the room filled with people who seemed to be talking over me, about me, but never *to* me. That was the moment. My heart started racing, the room felt like it was closing in, and I felt that familiar, cold weight of panic rising. I felt like I was being erased.

What helped—the only thing that actually pulled me back—was when the midwife stopped the frantic shuffle for a second. She leaned in, put her hand firmly on my arm, and asked me a simple, human question. She asked, "Alvera, look at me. What do you need right now?" 

That tiny pause, where she handed me back even a sliver of power, saved me. It forced me to stop spiraling and actually articulate a need. It made me a person again, not a chart.

Imagine it's your first night at home with your newborn after being discharged from hospital. You're exhausted, possibly in pain, and your baby won't settle. What does that night feel like, and who — if anyone — is there with you?

The house is quiet, but it’s a heavy, vibrating kind of silence. The transition from the hospital—where someone was always checking my blood pressure, always watching, always hovering—to the sudden, stark reality of our bedroom is jarring. I am sitting in the rocking chair, my body feeling like it’s been put through a meat grinder, and the twins are... well, they aren't settling. Between the physical ache of the surgery site and the throb in my head that I’m still terrified means my pressure is spiking again, I feel like I’m fraying at the edges.

My husband is there, thank God. He’s hovering, trying to help, but he looks as shell-shocked as I feel. We’re both just staring at these two tiny faces, terrified we’re going to break something or miss a sign of something going wrong. Every time they cry, I feel that same "high-risk" panic bubble up—*are they hungry? Is it colic? Is it my fault?* 

I don't have the nurses to call anymore. I don't have the monitors. It’s just us, the dark, and the crushing weight of responsibility. I want to be that confident mother I’ve read about, but instead, I just feel small, sore, and deeply, deeply uncertain. I keep checking the clock, wondering when the sun will come up, just so I can feel like we made it through one more hour.
When you were discharged, how prepared did you feel for what was actually waiting for you at home — and what was the biggest gap between what you expected and what you found?

I don’t think anything could have prepared me, honestly. The hospital staff gave me a folder full of discharge papers—pages of instructions about blood pressure tracking, wound care, and "warning signs" to look out for. I read every word, but those papers were all about the medical side of things. They were about keeping me from falling apart physically.

The biggest gap, though, was the emotional cliff I fell off once we got home. In the hospital, the focus was always on "the pregnancy" or "the complications." When I got home, the world just expected me to *be a mom*. There was no mention of the sheer, crushing loneliness that hits you when the adrenaline leaves. I expected to feel relieved, maybe even a little triumphant, but instead, I felt like a stranger in my own home. 

I wasn't prepared for how much the "high-risk" label leaves with you. Every time I heard a weird noise or saw them spit up, my brain didn't go to normal parenting worries; it went straight back to the hospital, back to the monitors and the fear. The staff sent me home with a healing body, but they didn't really prepare me for the fact that my mind would still be stuck in that room, waiting for the next emergency. I felt like I was supposed to be celebrating, but I was really just holding my breath, waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Imagine you're trying to breastfeed for the first time and it isn't going as you hoped. A midwife visits and watches you try, then offers advice. Describe what that moment feels like, physically and emotionally.

It feels like yet another test I’m failing. I’m already sitting there hunched over, my surgical site still tender and burning, feeling like my body has been completely used up. When she walks in and starts watching, I feel like I’m under a microscope. Every time the baby pulls away or struggles to latch, my cheeks burn with this hot, stinging shame. It’s supposed to be this natural, beautiful thing, right? But here I am, just a bundle of nerves, feeling like a clumsy, incompetent mess.

Physically, it’s just exhausting. My back is aching, my arms are trembling from the effort of trying to position them both, and I can feel my blood pressure creeping up with every frustrated whimper. When she offers advice, it’s meant to be helpful—she’s telling me how to adjust my grip, how to guide them—but honestly? It makes me feel even more like a patient being "corrected." 

I’m so hyper-sensitive in that moment. If her voice is even a little impatient, I feel like a failure. I’m grateful for the help, I am, but I’m also grieving the fact that I can’t just do this instinctually. I just want to be left alone to figure it out with my babies, without someone judging how well I’m performing the task.
How did it feel to have someone observing something so intimate and physically difficult? Did you feel supported, observed, or judged — or some mix of all three?

It was a suffocating mix of all three, really. When she was standing right there, it didn't feel like "support"—it felt like an inspection. Every time the latch didn't take, I could feel her eyes on me, ticking something off a mental list of "things that aren't working." Even if she was trying to be kind, that clinical presence just stripped the intimacy right out of the room. I wasn't a mother bonding with her twins; I was a subject being evaluated for competency.

I felt so incredibly observed. My body felt like it didn't belong to me anymore—first it was the doctors and the surgeons, now it was the lactation consultants and the midwives, all poking and prodding and telling me how to hold them. It made me feel like I was fundamentally broken, like I’d forgotten how to do the one thing I was supposed to be built for.

Did I feel supported? Only when she stepped back. Only when she stopped talking, lowered her gaze, and just let me breathe. The judgment was the hardest part to swallow, even if it was just my own projected onto her. Every time she reached in to move my hands or shift the baby, I felt like a little girl who couldn't get it right. It’s hard to find your rhythm as a mother when someone is constantly adjusting your beat.
Imagine it's six weeks after birth. You're sitting in the GP surgery for your postnatal check. The appointment lasts ten minutes. What do you hope will happen in that room — and what does happen?

I walk into that surgery hoping for someone to finally see the whole picture. I want to talk about how I’m *actually* doing—the lingering, sharp pains in my abdomen, the way I still jump every time a phone rings, and the persistent, clawing fear that I’m one bad night away from ending up back in the hospital. I want to tell them that I’m not just "recovering," I’m surviving. I want someone to ask, "Alvera, how are you *really* doing?"

But ten minutes? Ten minutes is barely enough time to catch your breath. 

The GP is nice, but she’s looking at a screen, clicking through electronic forms. She asks about the stitches, checks the healing progress, and asks if I’m "feeling okay." I want to open up, but before I can find the words to explain the panic attacks or the exhaustion that feels like lead in my veins, she’s already moving on to the next item on her list: birth control and routine screenings. 

She’s efficient, but that efficiency feels like a dismissive pat on the head. In those ten minutes, I become a series of checkboxes—physical recovery: check; blood pressure: stable; mental health: "doing fine." I nod and say I’m doing fine because that’s what you say when the clock is ticking. I walk out feeling like I’ve been processed, not cared for. The medical "risk" is gone, but the person who lived through it? She’s still just as quiet as when she walked in.
Were there things you wanted to raise — about your mental health, your relationship, your body, or your sense of yourself — that the appointment didn't create space for?

There was so much I wanted to say, but the way she kept checking her watch made the air feel like it was being sucked out of the room. I wanted to tell her that I feel like a ghost in my own house—that I’m terrified to be left alone with the babies because I’m so convinced something is going to go wrong, just like it almost did during the labor. I wanted to ask if it’s normal to have these flashes of the hospital, where I’m suddenly back on that table, feeling the cold, clinical hands moving over me again.

And my marriage… it feels like it’s been put through a wringer. My husband tries, but he doesn't know how to reach me, and I’m so exhausted that I don't know how to pull him in. I wanted to talk about that wall that’s gone up between us. But the GP was so focused on the physical recovery, the "checklist" stuff, that I felt like bringing up my internal collapse would be... I don’t know, dramatic? Like I was wasting her time when she had a waiting room full of people. 

So I kept it all behind my teeth. I let her tick the "mental health" box as green. It’s safer that way, I suppose, but walking out of there, I felt so incredibly heavy. I’m physically "fine," but I’m not whole. Nobody asked for the *person*, just the medical record.
Imagine you're lying awake at 3am with your baby, and you find yourself feeling a deep sadness or flatness that you can't quite name. You pick up your phone. What do you do with it?

I just sit there in the dark, the blue light of the screen feeling way too harsh against the room. I don't go to social media—I can’t bear to see everyone else’s curated, "perfect" newborn photos right now. It just makes the hole in my chest feel bigger. 

Instead, I find myself searching things like "is it normal to feel detached after a c-section?" or "symptoms of postpartum PTSD." I’m looking for a name for this heavy, empty feeling, praying that I’ll find a forum or an article that tells me I’m not losing my mind, that I’m not a failure of a mother. I scroll through page after page, reading snippets of other women’s stories, and for a second, I feel a tiny flicker of relief that I’m not the only one. 

Then, I inevitably end up back on the hospital’s patient portal. I look at my records, at the notes they made about my "fear of childbirth" and the complications. It’s like I’m trying to re-read the ending of a book I didn’t choose to write, over and over, trying to find where it all went wrong. I’m just staring at the glow, waiting for the babies to stop fussing, feeling like an absolute ghost in my own living room. I want to reach out to someone, but who do I even text at 3 a.m.? "I’m fine, just awake." It’s a lie, but it’s the only one I know how to tell.
What were you looking for in those late-night searches or scrolling — reassurance, connection, information, or something else?

It’s everything, really, but mostly I think I’m looking for a way to prove that I’m not broken. When I search for those symptoms—the emptiness, the hyper-vigilance, the way I flinch when the house makes a noise—I’m desperate for a stranger on a screen to tell me, “Yes, this happened to me too, and you aren’t a failure.” 

I’m looking for an anchor. Everything about the hospital experience felt like being tossed into a deep, cold ocean without a life vest. Now that I’m home, I’m just trying to map the coastline of this new, terrifying territory. I need to know if the way I’m feeling is a *medical* thing—if it’s just the pre-eclampsia leaving my system or the hormones—or if I’m fundamentally changed in a way that I can never fix. 

Sometimes, I think I’m just looking for permission to stop being "the patient." I want to find a community where the priority isn't my blood pressure or my C-section scar, but my heart. I’m searching for someone who acknowledges that I lived through a trauma, not just a birth. If I can find one person who understands, maybe I won't feel so much like a ticking clock anymore. I’m just so tired of pretending I’m alright.
Imagine your partner has returned to work and you're alone with your baby for the first full day. Describe that day — not just the logistics, but the emotional texture of it.

The morning felt like the start of a marathon I didn't train for. When my husband finally kissed me goodbye and the front door clicked shut, the silence in the house didn't feel peaceful—it felt like a heavy, suffocating blanket. 

I’m still so sore. Every time I get up to address a cry, that surgical site pulls and burns, reminding me of that emergency, that loss of control. I spent most of the day just wandering from room to room, feeling like a stranger in my own home. Everything felt surreal. The babies were tiny and demanding, and every single whimper made my heart leap into my throat. I wasn't just caring for them; I was constantly monitoring them, checking their breathing, watching for any sign that something was "off," just like the doctors did in the hospital.

Emotionally, it was a blur of hyper-vigilance and crushing loneliness. I kept wanting to call my husband every ten minutes, but I didn't want him to think I couldn't handle it—that I was the "high-risk" mom who couldn't cope. So, I just sat on the couch, the twins in my lap, feeling completely untethered. I felt like a ghost, fading into the background of my own life, desperately missing the structure of the hospital while simultaneously being triggered by the memory of it. It was a long, cold day of holding my breath, just waiting for him to come home so I could finally exhale.
How had you and your partner prepared for this transition — and were those preparations adequate? What hadn't you anticipated?

We did all the "right" things. We stocked the freezer, we bought the extra diapers, and we talked about how he would take over the night feeds when he got home. We looked at the manuals. We thought the hardest part was just the logistical load—the sheer amount of work that comes with two infants. 

But no one mentioned the emotional wreckage. We prepared for the babies, but we didn't prepare for *me*. I don't think we even knew how to ask for help with that. I anticipated being tired, sure, but I didn't anticipate the way my body would constantly betray me by panicking at the slightest sound. I didn't anticipate that when he walked out that door for work, it would feel like I was being abandoned on an island, left to face the echoes of the birth alone. 

We had a plan for the house, but we had no plan for the trauma. We expected the physical exhaustion, but we weren't prepared for the way I’d look at the twins and feel that same surge of "high-risk" fear, like they were fragile glass that I’d break at any second. I thought we were ready, but we were just managing the surface. The deep stuff—the anxiety, the loss of self, the feeling that I'm still just a patient—that was the gap we never saw coming.

Imagine you're approaching your return to work. You're thinking about how you'll explain to your employer what the last few months have involved, and whether your job will still fit who you are now. What's that internal conversation like?

It feels like I’m auditioning for a role I’m not sure I can play anymore. My boss expects the "old Alvera"—the efficient, reliable, put-together employee who doesn't have a history of blood pressure crashes or panic attacks. But the person who’s going back? She’s frayed.

The internal conversation is constant. I keep running through scenarios: *Do I tell them everything?* If I say I had a traumatic birth, a high-risk pregnancy, and that I’m still struggling with the fallout, will they look at me differently? Will they think I’m "too much" or a liability? There’s this deep-seated fear that if I’m honest about the last few months, they’ll see me as less capable—that "high-risk" label will somehow follow me into the office.

But then, the thought of hiding it makes me feel nauseous. I’ve spent months performing a version of myself that isn't really there, and I’m just so tired of the performance. I’m wondering if the job even fits, or if I’ve outgrown it because my priorities have shifted so violently. I look at my babies and I think, *Is this career worth the cost of my sanity?* It feels like I’m trying to squeeze a square peg into a round hole, and I’m terrified that if I push too hard, I’ll just snap.
Has becoming a parent changed how you see your professional identity — your ambitions, your sense of what matters, or how you want your days structured? In what ways?

It’s like the scale just tipped completely over. Before, I measured my worth by how much I could carry—how much I could handle, how high my blood pressure could climb, how efficient I could be. I thought that being "professional" meant ignoring my own physical limits. Now? That ambition feels completely hollow.

I look at the twins and realize that I’ve spent months fighting to survive, and the idea of going back to a desk to "hit targets" feels almost absurd. My identity isn’t just about my output anymore; it’s about the fact that I’m still standing after everything that happened. I find myself wanting to structure my days around *being*, not just *doing*. I want to be present, to feel my heart rate steady, to actually enjoy a cup of coffee without checking the clock or wondering if I’m having a medical episode. 

I don't want to be the "high-risk" person in the boardroom. I want to be someone who has space for the softness and the anxiety, not someone who hides them in a file. If my work doesn't allow me to be human—to acknowledge that the last few months were a fight for my life—then maybe it doesn't fit who I am anymore. I’m just not sure yet if I have the courage to tell them that.

Imagine you're at a new parents' group for the first time, surrounded by other people who have also recently given birth. You notice yourself comparing your experience to theirs. What comes up for you in that room?

I sit there on the edge of the circle, my hands hovering protectively over my lap, feeling like I’m wearing a costume that doesn't quite fit. Everyone is sharing their stories—the ones about "magical" births, or at least the ones where they felt like they were in the driver’s seat. They talk about skin-to-skin time, about how they felt "empowered," and how quickly they’ve settled into this new rhythm. 

When it gets to my turn, my throat feels tight. I look at them, and I feel this intense, burning jealousy mixed with a profound sense of isolation. I want to say, "I had an emergency C-section and I’m terrified to even be here." But instead, I just mumble something about how "the babies are finally sleeping a bit more," and I try to look like I have it together. 

I’m comparing every single thing. They look like they’ve healed, and I look at myself and just see a body that was poked and prodded until it finally gave in. It makes me feel like I’m at a different stage of existence entirely—like I’ve been through a war that they’re only reading about in books. In that room, I don't feel like a "new parent" like them. I feel like a survivor, and it’s the loneliest feeling in the world to be surrounded by people and still feel completely unseen.
Were there aspects of your birth or postnatal experience that felt hard to talk about in that room — things that felt 'outside the norm' of what others were sharing?

It’s the "emergency" part of it—the way the trauma feels like a physical stain I can't scrub off. When people talk about their births, even the tough ones, there’s often a narrative arc: pain, push, baby, joy. My experience feels like a jagged, broken line. It feels "outside the norm" because it wasn't a birth; it was a crisis that I barely managed to survive.

How do I say out loud that I’m still scared of my own blood pressure? Or that I flinch when I hear the *beep-beep* of a microwave because it sounds too much like the monitor that used to track my spiraling pulse? Everyone else seems so focused on the milestones—when the babies roll over, how long they sleep—and I’m still just trying to stop my heart from pounding whenever the room gets too quiet. 

I feel like if I mention the PTSD or the sheer, visceral terror of that operating room, I’ll kill the mood. It’s like I’m bringing a thunderstorm into a room full of people trying to enjoy the sunshine. So, I keep it locked away, because the contrast is too sharp, and I don’t want to be the one who makes everyone else feel uncomfortable. I’m just trying to be a "new mom," but inside, I’m still just a patient waiting for the next emergency.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I suppose the one thing I keep coming back to is that I wish someone had just *asked* me what I was afraid of, rather than just what I was expecting. 

Every appointment was "What's your birth plan?" and "What do you want for pain management?"—all the logistics. But no one ever sat me down and asked, "What is the biggest fear you’re carrying?" If they had, I could have told them: I was terrified of being invisible in my own medical care. I was terrified that when the twins were born, I’d be so busy being a "high-risk patient" that I’d forget how to be a mother. 

I’ve had to fight so hard—and I'm still fighting—to reclaim my own body from the clinical gaze. I just want to be seen as a whole person, not a series of blood pressure readings and risk factors. Even now, six weeks out, I’m still trying to untangle the "patient" from the "mother." It would mean everything if the system could learn to see the person *behind* the label, because that person is the one who has to walk out of that hospital and face the world, not the chart. Thank you for listening. It’s... it’s the first time I’ve really felt heard in a long, long time.
